requirement: Formal RFC Process: Require documented Request for Change (RFC) submissions for all production modifications.
Testing & Validation: Mandate pre-deployment testing, rollback, and reproducibility verification.
Segregation of Duties: Enforce SoD to prevent self-approval.
Model Lifecycle Control: Track model versioning, dataset hashes, and lineage metadata for AI systems.
Risk Evaluation: Classify changes (standard, normal, emergency) with defined approval chains.
Post-Implementation Review: Conduct retrospective analysis for failed or emergency changes.
Automation: Integrate change control into CI/CD pipelines and IaC workflows.
Configuration Drift Detection: Implement automated monitoring tools to detect and alert on unauthorized changes (Drift) that occur outside the formal change management process.
Standardized AI Lifecycle Stages: (ideation → deployment → monitoring).
Intake Forms & Risk Evaluation: Mandate intake forms with ethical-risk evaluation.
Documentation Requirements: Require model cards, data sheets, and fairness/robustness testing reports.
Maintain traceability across data, code, and model artifacts.
Establish rollback and safe-deployment mechanisms.
Require independent validation prior to go-live.
Integrate continuous monitoring pipelines for drift detection.

Automated Policy Enforcement: Implement "Policy-as-Code" gates within the CI/CD pipeline to automatically block deployments that fail to meet governance criteria (e.g., missing test reports or failed security scans).
Policy & Standards: Document secure-development policy and coding standards.
Enforce secure secret management.
Enforce code review and change-approval workflows.
Require threat modeling and security testing (SAST, DAST, IAST).
Automate dependency scanning and SBOM generation.
Pipeline Integration: Integrate static/dynamic scans into CI/CD pipelines.

IDE-Based Security Analysis: Implement real-time security scanning plugins within the developer's Integrated Development Environment (IDE) to detect and remediate vulnerabilities during the coding phase.
Pre-Hire Screening: Conduct background checks proportionate to job risk.
Onboarding: Require confidentiality, acceptable-use, and ethics acknowledgments.
Access Management: Implement role-based provisioning tied to HR events.
Offboarding: Immediate access revocation, device return, and exit checklist completion.
Whistleblower Protection: Maintain confidential reporting channels.
Training: Provide annual security, privacy, and AI ethics training.
Insider Risk Management: Implement behavioral monitoring for high-risk roles (e.g., administrators, researchers) to detect anomalous activities indicative of insider threats or data exfiltration.
Charter Definition: Document mission, authority, roles, quorum, and decision rights.
Escalation Authority: Empower the committee to halt deployments pending resolution of ethical risk.
Membership Composition: Ensure cross-functional participation including legal, HR, engineering, data science, and external advisors.
Ethical Review Process: Evaluate bias, fairness, cultural impact, explainability, and proportionality.
Minutes & Records: Maintain evidence of reviews, decisions, and monitoring requirements.
Annual Effectiveness Review: Evaluate committee effectiveness and update charter.
Public Transparency Reporting: Publish an annual "AI Ethics Report" detailing the number of models reviewed, approved, rejected, and the key ethical challenges addressed.
Charter & Authority: Define committee scope for privacy oversight.
Review of PIAs/DPIAs: Approve privacy risk assessments and impact assessments for new systems.
Data Minimization Verification: Validate that designs meet data-minimization principles.
Cross-Border Transfers: Evaluate risks and safeguards for international data flows.
Privacy Violations: Review privacy incidents and root-cause analyses.
Quarterly Reporting: Provide insights to executive leadership.
Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs) Adoption: Mandate and oversee the adoption of advanced PETs (e.g., Differential Privacy, Synthetic Data) to mathematically minimize risk in data utilization.
Charter & Mandate: Define responsibilities for security posture, program reviews, and approval authority.
Review of KRIs/KPIs: Evaluate risk, vulnerability trends, patch metrics, and incident data.
Escalation Governance: Define severity criteria and response timelines.
Resource Planning: Approve investments in tools, talent, penetration testing, and monitoring.
Integration with AI Risk: Ensure cybersecurity threat modeling and drift-related monitoring includes AI components.
Annual Program Review: Evaluate effectiveness and document improvements.

Cyber-Physical & Safety Risk Integration: Expand oversight to include Operational Technology (OT) and Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), ensuring digital security decisions do not compromise physical safety (e.g., manufacturing robots, autonomous vehicles).

KPI Framework — Define standardized KPIs and KRIs aligned with strategic goals and risk appetite (e.g., MTTD, patch compliance, AI model drift rate, bias index, data-loss incidents, control-audit pass rates).
Ownership & Accountability — Assign KPI owners; document calculation logic and data sources.
Frequency & Reporting — Update dashboards monthly for operational metrics and quarterly for strategic summaries to the Board.
Thresholds & Alerts — Define tolerance bands; configure automatic alerts when deviations exceed risk thresholds.
Data Collection & Validation — Automate KPI data feeds from SIEM, GRC, and MLOps tools; validate source data integrity quarterly.
Visualization & Analytics — Provide interactive dashboards for executive and functional views with trend analytics.
Continuous Improvement — Review metric relevance annually; integrate new risk domains as technology evolves.

Leading vs. Lagging Balance: Mandate the inclusion of Leading Indicators (predictive metrics, e.g., "Training Completion %") alongside Lagging Indicators (outcome metrics, e.g., "Incident Count") to enable proactive management.
ROPA & Data Maps — Maintain comprehensive processing inventories (Records of Processing Activities).
Consent & DSAR Handling — Automate consent logs, subject requests, and deletion confirmations.
Retention & Disposal — Enforce policies ensuring timely deletion of personal data.
Integration with AI Governance — Link data usage to AI transparency and fairness obligations.
Privacy Training — Provide targeted modules to developers and data scientists.
Audit Readiness — Conduct annual internal privacy audits and maintain proof of compliance.
Privacy Engineering — Integrate anonymization, pseudonymization, and federated-learning techniques within data pipelines.
Automated Data Lineage: Implement technical scanning tools to generate dynamic data lineage graphs, proving the provenance and flow of sensitive data across complex AI pipelines.
Vendor Classification: Categorize vendors by criticality, data access, and AI system impact.
Due Diligence: Perform pre-contract risk assessments covering security, privacy, AI governance, and regulatory posture.
Contractual Controls: Require clauses for data protection, confidentiality, breach notification, model-usage limits, and audit rights.
Continuous Monitoring: Use automated third-party risk platforms, external-surface scanning, and adverse-media checks.
AI-Specific Controls — Validate vendor AI systems for fairness, explainability, safety, and compliance with applicable regulations.
Remediation Management — Track vendor findings and verification evidence.
Offboarding & Exit Controls — Ensure data deletion, return, access revocation, and secure disposition.
Incident Integration — Include third parties in incident-response workflows.

Supply Chain Transparency (SBOM/AIBOM): Mandate that vendors provide Software/AI Bill of Materials (SBOM/AIBOM) to identify vulnerable components (e.g., Log4j) or datasets embedded within their delivered solutions.
AI Risk Framework — Define risk categories spanning ethical, technical, security, privacy, and societal impact considerations.
AI Risk Register — Maintain a centralized register tracking risk tier, likelihood/impact, mitigation plan, and owner.
Human Oversight — Document escalation paths and responsible human controllers for decision checkpoints.
Lifecycle Risk Assessments — Conduct risk assessments at design, data selection, training, deployment, operation, and retirement.
TEVV Alignment — Integrate testing, evaluation, verification, and validation into risk calculations.
Bias & Harm Review — Evaluate potential harms to protected classes, edge-case populations, and contextual misuse.
Continuous Monitoring — Monitor drift, anomaly behavior, performance regressions, and data-quality changes.
Auditability — Maintain traceability for datasets, model versions, training decisions, and evaluation outcomes.
Shadow AI Discovery: Implement network and endpoint scanning to detect unauthorized use of external AI services (e.g., public LLMs) to bring "Shadow AI" usage into the managed governance framework.
Vulnerability Identification: Use automated scanning (SAST/DAST/SCA/IaC), penetration testing, and red-team exercises.
Risk Evaluation: Calculate likelihood/impact using qualitative and quantitative models.
Treatment Planning: Assign owners, deadlines, and remediation strategies; track residual risk.
Threat Modeling: Perform structured threat modeling for critical systems and AI-related components at least annually.
Security KRIs: Maintain Key Risk Indicators (patch compliance, MTTR, phishing-success rate, drift anomaly frequency).
Continuous Monitoring: Use SIEM, XDR, log analytics, and behavioral analytics.
Integration with AI Risks: Include adversarial attacks, model-exfiltration risk, dataset-integrity risks, and poisoning vectors.
Reporting: Provide updates to leadership, metrics for trends, and escalation for high-risk issues.

Automated Remediation (SOAR): Implement Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR) playbooks to automatically remediate common low-risk threats (e.g., blocking IPs, isolating hosts) without human intervention.
Escalation Procedures: Define immediate escalation thresholds for critical risks or noncompliance events.
Content Requirements: Include risk posture, incidents, performance metrics, TEVV results, and trend analysis.
Reporting Framework: Establish standardized templates for monthly, quarterly, and ad hoc reporting.
Audience Segmentation: Tailor reports for operational teams, management, and the Board.
External Reporting: Align customer assurance and regulatory reporting channels with privacy and cyber frameworks.
Record Retention: Archive reports for at least 3 years for auditability.
Automated Generation: Integrate with dashboards or GRC tools to automate data aggregation.

Executive Attestation (Sign-off): Require formal sign-off from the Accountable Executive (e.g., CISO, CDO) on critical reports, verifying they have reviewed the data and attest to its accuracy (Duty of Candor).
